stem: A Gauss\(-\)Legendre quadrature rule uses \( [[n]] \) nodes, with both the nodes and the weights chosen optimally. This rule integrates every polynomial exactly up to some maximal degree \( d \). What is the largest degree \( d \) of polynomial that the rule integrates exactly?
solution: An \( n \)-node Gauss\(-\)Legendre rule attains the maximal degree of exactness \( 2n - 1 \). With \( n = [[n]] \) nodes this gives \( d = 2([[n]]) - 1 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the degree of exactness \( n-1 \) of an interpolatory rule with arbitrary (non-optimal) nodes, such as Newton\(-\)Cotes.
This mistakes the degree of exactness for the number of nodes.
This is one more than the correct \( 2n-1 \).
This overshoots the optimal degree of exactness by two.
This undershoots by one.
This confuses the degree of exactness with \( n+1 \).

stem: A closed Newton\(-\)Cotes rule uses \( [[n]] \) equally spaced nodes (so \( [[n]]-1 \) panels), with weights chosen by exactly integrating the interpolating polynomial through those nodes. By the classical symmetry ("bonus degree") result, such a rule is exact for polynomials up to degree \( [[n]]-1 \) in general, but gains one extra degree of exactness when the number of panels is even. What is the exact degree of exactness \( d \) of this rule?
solution: With \( [[n]] \) nodes there are \( [[n]]-1 \) panels. If \( [[n]]-1 \) is even (i.e. \( n \) is odd), the rule gains a bonus degree, giving \( d = n \); otherwise \( d = n-1 \). Here this gives \( d = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This always assumes no bonus degree, missing the case where the number of panels is even.
This always assumes the bonus degree applies, even when the number of panels is odd.
This mistakes the Newton\(-\)Cotes formula for the optimal Gauss\(-\)Legendre formula \( 2n-1 \).
This overshoots by one beyond the correct degree.
This undershoots by one below the correct degree.
This is one more than the correct degree of exactness.

stem: A composite quadrature rule has global error \( O(h^{p}) \) with \( p = [[p]] \) (for example, the composite trapezoidal rule has \( p=2 \) and the composite Simpson's rule has \( p=4 \)). If the number of subintervals is increased by a factor of \( [[r]] \) (so \( h \to h/[[r]] \)), by what factor does the error decrease?
solution: Since the error scales like \( h^{p} \), shrinking \( h \) by a factor of \( [[r]] \) shrinks the error by a factor of \( [[r]]^{p} = [[r]]^{[[p]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies \( r \) and \( p \) instead of raising \( r \) to the power \( p \).
This uses one too few powers of \( r \).
This uses one too many powers of \( r \).
This swaps the roles of \( r \) and \( p \) in the exponent.
This is one more than the correct decrease factor.
This is one less than the correct decrease factor.

stem: Simpson's rule with the two subintervals \( [[[a]], [[c]]] \) and \( [[[c]], [[b]]] \) (midpoint \( c = [[c]] \)) is used to approximate \( \displaystyle\int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]} x^{4}\,dx \), namely \( S = \dfrac{[[b]]-[[a]]}{6}\big(f([[a]]) + 4f([[c]]) + f([[b]])\big) \) with \( f(x) = x^{4} \). Since Simpson's rule is exact only for polynomials up to degree 3, \( S \) will not equal the true integral. What is the error \( \displaystyle\int_{[[a]]}^{[[b]]} x^{4}\,dx - S \)?
solution: The exact integral is \( \dfrac{[[b]]^{5}-([[a]])^{5}}{5} \) and Simpson's estimate is \( S = \dfrac{[[b]]-([[a]])}{6}\big([[a]]^{4}+4([[c]])^{4}+[[b]]^{4}\big) \); subtracting gives the error \( [[answer]] \), which is nonzero precisely because \( x^{4} \) exceeds Simpson's degree-3 exactness.
distractor_rationales: This assumes Simpson's rule is exact for every polynomial, giving zero error.
This has the sign of the error reversed.
This reports the exact integral value itself, forgetting to subtract Simpson's estimate.
This reports Simpson's estimate itself, forgetting to subtract it from the exact integral.
This is one more than the correct error.
This is one less than the correct error.
